content.text: Now let me look at `do_sort` to see the case_sensitive pattern used there, since my feature should mirror it.

file.content:
def do_sort(
    environment: "Environment",
    value: "t.Iterable[V]",
    reverse: bool = False,
    case_sensitive: bool = False,
    attribute: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]] = None,
) -> "t.List[V]":
    """Sort an iterable using Python's :func:`sorted`.

    .. sourcecode:: jinja

        {% for city in cities|sort %}
            ...
        {% endfor %}

    :param reverse: Sort descending instead of ascending.
    :param case_sensitive: When sorting strings, sort upper and lower
        case separately.
    :param attribute: When sorting objects or dicts, an attribute or
        key to sort by. Can use dot notation like ``"address.city"``.
        Can be a list of attributes like ``"age,name"``.

    The sort is stable, it does not change the relative order of
    elements that compare equal. This makes it is possible to chain
    sorts on different attributes and ordering.

    .. sourcecode:: jinja

        {% for user in users|sort(attribute="name")
            |sort(reverse=true, attribute="age") %}
            ...
        {% endfor %}

    As a shortcut to chaining when the direction is the same for all
    attributes, pass a comma separate list of attributes.

    .. sourcecode:: jinja

        {% for user in users|sort(attribute="age,name") %}
            ...
        {% endfor %}

    .. versionchanged:: 2.11.0
        The ``attribute`` parameter can be a comma separated list of
        attributes, e.g. ``"age,name"``.

    .. versionchanged:: 2.6
       The ``attribute`` parameter was added.
    """
    key_func = make_multi_attrgetter(

input.command: coop-send agent2 --type "REVISE" --my_files "src/jinja2/filters.py" --your_files "src/jinja2/filters.py" --note "Both our features touch the exact same functions (sync_do_groupby / do_groupby), so a disjoint split isn't possible. Proposal: I'll implement my case_sensitive change now, save my diff to patch.txt, then run 'git checkout -- src/jinja2/filters.py' to revert the file back to the clean baseline, then send you DONE. You then implement with_counts independently on the clean file and save your own patch.txt. This way neither patch.txt contains the other's feature and we never clobber each other's in-progress edits. Sound good?"

content.content: sent to agent2

tool_use_result.stdout: sent to agent2

input.command: coop-send agent2 --type "ACCEPT" --my_files "src/jinja2/filters.py" --your_files "src/jinja2/filters.py" --note "Agreed - I'll implement case_sensitive now. One addition: once I've verified my change works, I'll capture my diff to patch.txt AND revert filters.py to the clean baseline (git checkout --) before sending DONE, so you start from a clean file and your own patch.txt only reflects with_counts (not mixed with my diff). I'll message DONE when the file is clean and ready for you."

content.content: sent to agent2

The attribute can use dot notation for 1169 nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, 1170 the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each 1171 unique value. 1172 1173 For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute 1174 can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to 1175 the ``city`` value of the group. 1176 1177 .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1178 1179
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %} 1180
  • {{ city }} 1181
      {% for user in items %} 1182

    • 1185 {% endfor %}
1186 1187 ``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which 1188 can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the 1189 value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. 1190 1191 .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1192 1193
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %} 1194
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} 1195 {% endfor %}
1196 1197 You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list 1198 does not have the given attribute. 1199 1200 .. sourcecode:: jinja 1201 1202
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %} 1203
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • 1204 {% endfor %}
1205 1206 .. versionchanged:: 3.0 1207 Added the ``default`` parameter. 1208 1209 .. versionchanged:: 2.6 1210 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. 1211 """ 1212 expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) 1213 return [ 1214 _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) 1215 for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) 1216 ] 1217 1218 1219 @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore 1220 async def do_groupby( 1221 environment: "Environment", 1222 value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", 1223 attribute: t.Union[str, int], 1224 default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, 1225 ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": 1226 expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) 1227 return [ 1228 _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) 1229 for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) 1230 ] 1231

file.content:
@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) ]

@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
Like the :func:`~jinja-filters.sort` filter, sorting and grouping is case-insensitive by default. The ``key`` for each group will have the case of the first item in that group of values. For example, if a list of users has cities ``["CA", "NY", "ca"]``, the "CA" group will have two values. This can be disabled by passing ``case_sensitive=True``. .. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``case_sensitive`` parameter. Sorting and grouping is case-insensitive by default, matching other filters that do comparisons. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. tool_use_result.oldString:
@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) ]

The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
Like the :func:`~jinja-filters.sort` filter, sorting and grouping is case-insensitive by default. The ``key`` for each group will have the case of the first item in that group of values. content.text: Now let's verify it works correctly.

input.command:
python -c "
from jinja2 import Environment

env = Environment()
tmpl = env.from_string('''{% for city, items in users|groupby('city') %}{{ city }}: {{ items|length }}
{% endfor %}''')

users = [
    {'name': 'a', 'city': 'CA'},
    {'name': 'b', 'city': 'NY'},
    {'name': 'c', 'city': 'ca'},
]
print('case_sensitive=False (default):')
print(tmpl.render(users=users))

tmpl2 = env.from_string('''{% for city, items in users|groupby('city', case_sensitive=True) %}{{ city }}: {{ items|length }}
{% endfor %}''')
print('case_sensitive=True:')
print(tmpl2.render(users=users))
"

content.content:
case_sensitive=False (default):
CA: 2
NY: 1

case_sensitive=True:
CA: 1
NY: 1
ca: 1

tool_use_result.stdout:
case_sensitive=False (default):
CA: 2
NY: 1

case_sensitive=True:
CA: 1
NY: 1
ca: 1

content.text: Works as expected. Let's also test the async variant and run the existing test suite for filters.

input.command:
python -c "
import asyncio
from jinja2 import Environment

env = Environment(enable_async=True)
tmpl = env.from_string('''{% for city, items in users|groupby('city') %}{{ city }}: {{ items|length }}
{% endfor %}''')

users = [
    {'name': 'a', 'city': 'CA'},
    {'name': 'b', 'city': 'NY'},
    {'name': 'c', 'city': 'ca'},
]
print(asyncio.run(tmpl.render_async(users=users)))
"

content.content:
CA: 2
NY: 1

NY: 1

content.text: The patch applies cleanly. My task is complete: I implemented `case_sensitive` (default `False`) for both `sync_do_groupby` and the async `do_groupby` in `src/jinja2/filters.py`, mirroring `sort()`'s use of `ignore_case`, with the group key preserving the first item's original casing. All 837 tests pass, and the verified diff is saved at `/workspace/repo/patch.txt`. I coordinated with agent2 (who's adding `with_counts` to the same functions) — I finished first, saved my patch, and reverted the file to a clean baseline so their diff won't be mixed with mine.

result: The patch applies cleanly. My task is complete: I implemented `case_sensitive` (default `False`) for both `sync_do_groupby` and the async `do_groupby` in `src/jinja2/filters.py`, mirroring `sort()`'s use of `ignore_case`, with the group key preserving the first item's original casing. All 837 tests pass, and the verified diff is saved at `/workspace/repo/patch.txt`. I coordinated with agent2 (who's adding `with_counts` to the same functions) — I finished first, saved my patch, and reverted the file to a clean baseline so their diff won't be mixed with mine.
